heading: NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CXLIX
card_summary: This chunk consists of Campanini's endnotes (nn. 304–356) to his introductory essay on De harmonia mundi, covering a dense range of topics: the Kabbalistic "death by kiss" (mors osculi), Zorzi's use of Hermes Trismegistus and the Quadripartitus Hermetis, his handling of Messianic prophecy in Isaiah, the Karaites as a Jewish sectarian precedent for Scripture-only exegesis, and — most extensively — Zorzi's doctrine that Hebrew is the primordial, celestial, and eschatological language. Key figures discussed include Ficino, Pico della Mirandola, Agrippa, Augustine, Abraham de Balmes, and the printer Daniel Bomberg. Campanini cross-references his own earlier articles and provides substantial bibliographic apparatus for modern scholarship.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 34 is entirely composed of Campanini's endnotes (numbered 304–356) to the introductory essay of his critical edition of Zorzi's *De harmonia mundi*. Despite the apparatus format, the notes are substantively rich: they quote extended Latin passages from Zorzi, identify his sources, correct his errors, and situate his ideas within both the Renaissance intellectual tradition and modern scholarship. The central themes are (1) esoteric death-mysticism (*mors osculi*), (2) Zorzi's selective use of Hermetic sources, (3) Messianic exegesis of Isaiah, (4) the Karaites as a polemical reference point, and (5) — most extensively — the theology of Hebrew as the primordial and eschatological language.

---

## The "Death by Kiss" (Mors Osculi) — Note 304

Note 304 cross-references multiple loci in *De harmonia mundi* (1,5,15; 1,7,24; 3,7,18) and the *Problemata* (1,6,372) for Zorzi's treatment of the Kabbalistic concept of *mors osculi* — the "death of the kiss." Campanini quotes the relevant Latin directly:

> Quam mortem Propheta vocat sanctorum mortem, et preciosam in conspectu Domini. Hebraei vero vocant ipsam osculi mortem. Et haec mors est excessus aut raptus quidam mentis, per separationem animae a corpore, non autem corporis ab anima.

<cite>De harmonia mundi, Problemata 1,6,372, cited in note 304 (p. 149)</cite>

This is the death described in Psalm 116:15 ("Precious in the sight of the Lord is the death of His saints"), which the Hebrews identify as *mitat neshiqah* — death by divine kiss, meaning ecstatic mystical union rather than bodily dissolution. Campanini connects this to Pico della Mirandola's *Conclusiones* (second series, no. 11) and directs the reader to Wirszubski (1990) and Fishbane (1994) for further context.

---

## Hermetic Sources: The Quadripartitus and Agrippa — Notes 319–321

Notes 319–321 address Zorzi's use of the *Quadripartitus Hermetis*, a Hermetic text on the correspondence of fixed stars, precious stones, and plants. Campanini notes that Zorzi reproduces the essentials of this text in *De harmonia mundi* 1,4,31, comparing it with Ficino's treatment in *De vita libri tres* III,8. Crucially, Campanini observes that both Ficino and Zorzi strategically omit the fourth section of the *Quadripartitus* — the section on astromagical characters — to avoid the charge of necromancy. Agrippa, by contrast, already in the 1510 manuscript of *De occulta philosophia*, cites all four sections without hesitation.

Note 320 also catches a curious error: in the *Problemata* (2,3,149), Zorzi misidentifies the title of the Hermetic tract, apparently confusing it with the *De lapidibus filiorum Israel* attributed to the Sabean sage Thabit ibn Qurra.

---

## Messianic Prophecy and the Polemic Against Jewish Exegesis — Notes 323–338

Notes 323–338 deal with Zorzi's Christological reading of Isaiah 9, particularly the title "Prince of Peace" (*princeps pacis*). Campanini quotes an extended passage from *De harmonia mundi* 2,7,2 in which Zorzi argues against the Jewish interpretation that Isaiah's prophecy was fulfilled in Hezekiah:

> Isaias quoque ait: Et factus est principatus super humeros eus, et vocabitur admirabilis consilii angelus, vel admirabilis consiliarius, Deus fortis, Pater futuri saeculi [...] Ex quo ridendi sunt scioli Hebraeoroum, qui obstinatione excaecati, qui dicunt vaticinium illud in Ezechia esse completum, cum nec Ezechiae, nec Davidis regnum, familiave secundum carnem duret, immo regalis illa prosapia olim penitus extincta fuit, ut Iosephus et Eusebius narrant.

<cite>De harmonia mundi 2,7,2, cited in note 326 (p. 150)</cite>

Zorzi's argument is that David's royal line was extinguished in the flesh — as Josephus and Eusebius confirm — leaving only "the poor family of Christ," which was excluded from worldly kingship precisely because it aspired to a different kingdom. This is classic Christian supersessionist typology.

Note 332 introduces the **Karaites** (*Carieni* in Zorzi's Latin), explaining that they took their name from *miqra* (Scripture) or the root *qara'* ("to read/proclaim"). Campanini quotes Zorzi's own definition from *De harmonia mundi* 2,4,11:

> Fuerunt quidem non solum hac tempestate, sed longe antiquiores, etiam apud Hebraeos, qui asserere voluerunt solam fidem sufficere ad salutem: et illi dicti sunt Rabini quia sequebantur opiniones quorundam magistrorum; contra quos ex adverso insurrexit alia secta, quae dicebatur Carienorum, quia sequebatur tantummodo lectionem et textum scripturae sacrae nullas admittendo opiniones hominum.

<cite>De harmonia mundi 2,4,11, cited in note 333 (p. 150)</cite>

Zorzi thus maps a Jewish internal dispute (Rabbanites vs. Karaites) onto a Christian theological debate about Scripture and tradition, anticipating the Protestant controversy. Note 334 confirms that in the *Problemata*, this polemic against the "theologi aquilonares" (Lutherans) becomes fully explicit.

---

## Hebrew as the Primordial and Eschatological Language — Notes 341–356

The most extended section of this chunk concerns Zorzi's doctrine of the Hebrew language, concentrated in notes 341–356. Note 341 quotes *De harmonia mundi* 2,2,13 on Hebrew's superiority over Greek and Latin:

> Imitari enim voluit Plato etymologiam primae linguae, in qua protoplastes nomina rebus imposuit accomodatissima. Sed tantum differt convenientia nominum cum rebus in lingua Hebraea ab ea, quae est in idiomate Greaco et Latino, quanto ipsa lingua Hebraea sacratior et divinior est istis, quae illam prosequuntur, longis tamen passibus, et longioribus Latina quam Graeca, quia haec immediate ab Hebraea, Latina autem a Graeca deducta est.

<cite>De harmonia mundi 2,2,13, cited in note 341 (p. 151)</cite>

Zorzi claims that Plato himself, in his etymological investigations, was imitating the "first language" (*prima lingua*) in which Adam named all things with perfect aptness. Hebrew's superiority is both ontological (its names fit things more exactly) and genealogical (Greek derives from Hebrew, Latin from Greek).

Note 349 contains the most elaborate treatment, quoting an extended passage from *De harmonia mundi* 3,8,11,2 on Hebrew as the language of heaven, the language of the Law, of Christ's preaching, of the Psalms, and of all authentic oracles. Zorzi argues that the Hebrew alphabet itself was formed in the pattern of the celestial figures written on the throne of God, and that Moses used these letters when giving the Law. The eschatological implication is that just as Hebrew was the first language before the Babel confusion, it will be the last — restored in the heavenly city:

> Convenientissimum igitur est, ut sicut quelibet victoriosa civitas non solum iugum imponit domitis gentibus, sed multoties et idioma, sic Imperator illius civitatis supernae, et astantes principes decernent, ut omnes illuc evecti loquantur lingua, et sermone inde oriundo, et quo solebat uti princeps illius regni, dum inter homines versaretur.

<cite>De harmonia mundi 3,8,11,2, cited in note 349 (pp. 151–152)</cite>

The argument draws on Augustine (*De civitate Dei* 16,3; 16,11; 19,7) for the preservation of Hebrew in the line of Heber and the condemnation of Babel's linguistic confusion as punishment for pride. The note also makes the etymological claim that Abraham was the first person in Scripture called "Hebrew" (*Hebraeus*), derived from the root *habar* (to cross over), as Zorzi elaborates elsewhere.

Note 350 identifies the Venetian printing context: Abraham de Balmes's *Peculium Abrae* was published posthumously by Daniel Bomberg in Venice in 1523, and note 351 links Zorzi's linguistic theology to De Balmes's grammar, citing *De harmonia mundi* 3,6,8 where Zorzi appeals to Hebrew etymology (*teshuvah* as conversion rather than punishment) as evidence of the "more perfect language."

Notes 353–355 cite Campanini's own earlier research (1997, 1998) on the celestial origin of the Hebrew alphabet, cross-referencing Nachmanides's commentary on *Sefer Yetzirah* and the *Sefer ha-Peli'ah* as Kabbalistic sources Zorzi may have consulted.

---

## Editorial and Bibliographic Dimension

Note 322 explicitly flags that the two sections of the introduction dealing with Messianic prophecy and Hebrew represent "una versione rielaborata ed emendata" of Campanini's 1999 article — valuable transparency about the essay's genesis. The chunk as a whole reflects Campanini's exceptionally wide-ranging apparatus: he cites Kristeller, Chastel, Walker, Vasoli, and Stausberg on Ficino; Secret and Vasoli on Hermetism; Lucentini and Perrone Compagni on Hermetic textual tradition; and his own prior publications at multiple points.
